summary: Cross-chapter audit of cycle 02 snapshot (65,282 prose words, 18 chapters). REDUNDANCY: 14 character re-introduction and setting re-description findings from the mandatory comparison protocol, plus 10 findings from density/counting checks covering proof-of-baseline, image density, hedge density, and near-verbatim passages. Em-dash density (26/2390 = 1.1%) is well below the 10% threshold — no finding emitted. Sentence-opener monotony: no single pronoun opener exceeds 7% manuscript-wide (highest: 'I' at 5.7%, 'He' at 5.6%) — no finding emitted. CONSISTENCY: 11 findings across character details, objects, quantitative continuity, and the fire document. The most critical consistency issue is that Martin's fire document is read in Ch8 and Ch16 as the same physical document but contains substantially different accounts of the fire (different date, different cause, different location of victim, different fire ruling). Nine additional factual contradictions were found (cash amount, car make, photo count, draft board columns, envelope size, safe contents inventory).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 line 45 establishes Cliff: 'Silver hair, permanent tan, the kind of posture that made you aware of your own' in 'pressed chinos and a collared polo.' Ch8 line 2090 re-introduces all three traits in a mirror scene: 'Silver hair he paid sixty bucks to keep neat at a barber in Edina. Permanent tan from property lines and half-built decks. Collared polo bought on a maxed-out credit card.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 line 2090 delivers a near-complete re-introduction of every physical trait from Ch1/45 (silver hair, permanent tan, collared polo) seven chapters later. First establishment: Ch1 line 45.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or heavily compress the mirror passage. Retain only new information (Tag Heuer, maxed credit card, gap between self-image and reality). Let one brief sensory detail carry appearance rather than re-cataloging all three Ch1 traits.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 mirror passage no longer names silver hair, permanent tan, or collared polo. New financial-stress details survive.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8 line 2076: 'Tommy Renaldi. Forty-seven. High school football coach. PE teacher. Married, three kids.' Ch11 line 2761: 'Tommy Renaldi. High school football coach. Married to Denise. Three kids.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch11 line 2761 repeats Tommy's biographical capsule nearly verbatim from Ch8/2076 (coach, married, three kids). A third identical capsule will appear at Ch18/4672. First establishment of this capsule: Ch8 line 2076.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the capsule at line 2761 with Paul's emotional reaction to the will naming Tommy. Paul already knows who Tommy is; the passage should show Paul's confusion about why Martin chose Tommy, not re-teach the reader Tommy's bio.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 no longer lists Tommy's age, job title, marital status, or number of children in a summary capsule.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8 line 2076: 'Tommy Renaldi. Forty-seven. High school football coach. PE teacher. Married, three kids.' Ch18 line 4672: 'Tommy Renaldi was forty-seven, a high school football coach and PE teacher out in Apple Valley, married with three kids.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch18/4672 is the third occurrence of Tommy's biographical capsule (first Ch8/2076, second Ch11/2761). By chapter 18 the reader has known Tommy's occupation, age, and family for ten chapters.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the biographical capsule. Keep 'living a whole adult life built around being loud enough and simple enough that nobody thought to look under it' — that is new insight. Mallick can reflect on Tommy through specific investigative observations rather than his resume.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch18 does not list Tommy's age, job title, or family size in a summary capsule.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1/45 establishes Cliff's outfit. Ch13/3508: 'Pressed chinos, navy polo, and the leather loafers he wore indoors.' Ch13/3518 (10 lines later): 'Chinos, polo, loafers making a soft sound on the hardwood.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 catalogs Cliff's outfit twice within 10 lines (3508 and 3518), both echoing the Ch1/45 establishment. The outfit has also been re-described in Ch7/1841 and Ch8/2090. First establishment: Ch1 line 45.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the clothing inventory at line 3508. At line 3518, keep only the loafer sound on hardwood — fresh sensory detail. The reader does not need Cliff's outfit listed again.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 mentions Cliff's clothing at most once, through sensory detail rather than inventory.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1/45: 'pressed chinos and a collared polo... Silver hair, permanent tan.' Ch7/1841: 'Cliff came in from the hallway showered and pressed, fresh polo, chinos, hair combed... careful tanned hands.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch7/1841 re-describes Cliff's outfit (polo, chinos) and tan from Ch1/45, one chapter before Ch8 delivers the full mirror re-introduction, creating a two-chapter cluster of Cliff re-description.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the outfit list with a single detail showing Cliff's incongruity — the fact that he looks ready for a client meeting while everyone else looks wrecked. Cut 'polo, chinos, hair combed' and 'tanned hands.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch7 does not list Cliff's polo, chinos, or tan. Cliff's appearance is conveyed through contrast.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7/1857: 'Fifteen years in pharmaceutical sales.' Ch13 lines 3504, 3550, 3562, 3568: four separate references to Doug's pharmaceutical background ('pharmaceutical sales calls,' 'pharmaceutical vocabulary,' 'pharmaceutical training,' 'pharmaceutical sales voice').
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 invokes Doug's pharmaceutical career four times within a single chapter after it was established in Ch7/1857. The repetition converts a character detail into a verbal tic.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the strongest single pharmaceutical reference in Ch13 (line 3504, tied to Doug's observational habit). Cut or rephrase the other three to refer to Doug's training or knowledge without repeating the word 'pharmaceutical.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 uses the word 'pharmaceutical' at most once.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1/9 establishes the lake house: 'an A-frame on Elbow Lake, pine-paneled, with a deck wrapping around the lake side.' Ch8/1960 re-inventories: 'Cedar siding, original but maintained. Wraparound deck... Vaulted A-frame ceiling... Lake frontage, eighty feet, mature pines and birch, dock in decent shape.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 opening (line 1960) re-describes the entire lake house from scratch through Cliff's appraisal lens: A-frame, wraparound deck, pines, birch, dock, fireplace, lake frontage. Every element was established in Ch1.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep Cliff's appraisal compulsion but appraise what is NEW or deteriorating — the deferred maintenance, the gap between value and what Cliff needs. Cut the re-catalog of A-frame, pines, lake frontage, and fireplace.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 opening does not re-describe A-frame shape, wraparound deck layout, pines, or lake frontage.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Refrigerator cycling established Ch1/13 and re-described in Ch2/594, Ch4/922, Ch5/1286, Ch5/1391, Ch7/1955, Ch8/1994, Ch9/2135. Ch10/2472 re-introduces from scratch: 'The kitchen in Martin Kessler's lake house had the sort of refrigerator that announced itself. Every forty minutes or so the compressor kicked on with a shudder that rattled the glasses in the cabinet above the sink. Mallick had timed it.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch10/2472 re-introduces the refrigerator as if it is new information, giving it a full expository paragraph after 8 prior chapters have established the cycling hum. 'Mallick had timed it' is the only new detail.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the expository paragraph with a single sentence: Mallick noting the time via the fridge cycle count. Cut the description of compressor sound, glass rattling, and 40-minute interval.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 opening references the refrigerator in one sentence or less. The compressor sound and 40-minute cycle are not re-explained.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4/916: 'The draft board stayed on the wall, Martin's picks circled in red Sharpie.' Ch5/1315: 'the draft board on the wall. Martin Kessler's picks circled in red marker.' Consecutive chapters repeat the same image in nearly identical phrasing.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch5/1315 repeats the draft-board-with-red-circled-picks image from Ch4/916 one chapter later. Ch6/1782 repeats it a third consecutive time. First establishment as post-death artifact: Ch4/916.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch5, cut the draft board description. Mallick can register the room without the board, or notice something only a detective would notice about it.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5 does not describe Martin's picks circled in red on the draft board.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4/916: 'Martin's picks circled in red Sharpie.' Ch5/1315: 'Martin Kessler's picks circled in red marker.' Ch6/1782: 'the draft board still hanging on the great room wall, Martin's picks circled in red marker.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch6/1782 is the third consecutive chapter to describe the draft board with Martin's picks circled in red.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the draft board description at Ch6/1782. If the board must be referenced, use a single word ('the board') without the red-picks detail.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch6 does not describe Martin's picks circled in red on the draft board.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8/1986: 'Martin's wall safe behind a framed lake landscape print.' Ch10/2488: 'The wall safe in Martin's study was hidden behind a lake landscape print.' Ch11/2733: 'the same lake landscape print Martin's parents had bought at a craft fair in Duluth in 1983.' Three consecutive chapters describe the safe-behind-painting setup.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch11/2733 is the third chapter in a row to describe the safe behind the lake print. The Duluth craft fair provenance is repeated verbatim in Ch17/4196.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Paul already knows about the safe. Skip the print description — he can access the safe directly. The Duluth provenance can stay here if cut from Ch17.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 does not re-describe the lake landscape print as a discovery.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11/2733: 'the same lake landscape print Martin's parents had bought at a craft fair in Duluth in 1983.' Ch17/4196: 'a lake scene Martin's parents had bought at a craft fair in Duluth in 1983 and kept because the frame matched the paneling.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch17/4196 repeats the Duluth craft fair provenance of the lake print nearly verbatim from Ch11/2733.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the Duluth provenance at Ch17/4196. Paul can lift the print off the hook without re-narrating its origin.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 does not mention Duluth, craft fair, or 1983 in connection with the lake print.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4/916: 'The great room still smelled like Martin's scotch, the Lagavulin he'd poured too generously last night.' Ch7/1809: 'Martin's Lagavulin still hung in the great room, smoke and iodine caught in the paneling.' Ch9/2135: 'Martin's scotch, the Lagavulin, still sweetened the air in the great room beyond the kitchen.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The Lagavulin-scent-in-the-great-room is re-described in nearly identical terms three times (Ch4, Ch7, Ch9). Ch9 is only two chapters after Ch7.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the Ch4 establishment. In Ch9/2135, cut the Lagavulin smell or replace it with a sensory detail showing time passing (the smell fading, the bottle depleting).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9 does not re-describe the Lagavulin scent as still present in the great room.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1/9: 'wood smoke and lake damp lived in the pine.' Ch13/3508: 'pine paneling, lake damp, the ghost of Martin's presence that lived in the walls the way old smoke does.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13/3508 echoes Ch1/9 almost verbatim in structure, word choice, and metaphor ('lived in the pine/walls,' three-element list of pine/damp/smoke).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or rephrase the sensory catalog at Ch13/3508 to use different imagery from Ch1.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 does not list pine paneling, lake damp, and smoke together.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Paul's insurance identity is invoked 22 times across 10 chapters. Ch4/1024: 'insurance-adjuster precision.' Ch5/1327: self-identification in interview (functional). Ch7/1805: 'that insurance face.' Ch8/1970: 'Insurance claims, probably. Paul adjusted claims the way other people bit their nails.' Ch7 and Ch8 are consecutive chapters both invoking insurance as a character shorthand.
redundancy_findings.problem: Paul's insurance/claims-adjuster identity is re-proved via metaphor in Ch8/1970 ('adjusted claims the way other people bit their nails') one chapter after the same metaphor pattern in Ch7/1805 ('that insurance face'). The adjacent-chapter cluster re-proves the baseline without new leverage.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch8/1970, describe Paul's behavior at the table without the insurance metaphor. Let the reader recognize Paul's precision from prior chapters rather than being re-told its source.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 does not use 'insurance,' 'claims,' or 'adjuster' as a metaphor for Paul's behavior.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1/27 establishes Doug's weight loss: 'Doug had lost weight. More than the usual. His face was thinner, the hoodie hung wrong on him, and the bags under his eyes looked permanent.' Ch2/628 re-proves: 'hollowed, the bags under his eyes almost purple in the kitchen light, the weight loss that nobody was asking about.' Consecutive chapters both describing the same deterioration.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch2/628 re-describes Doug's weight loss and under-eye bags one chapter after Ch1/27 established them. The observation adds no new information — both describe the same visual from different POVs.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch2/628, cut the physical re-description of Doug's weight loss. Paul can register Doug's state through behavior (sitting apart, not eating) rather than re-cataloging the same physical details Nate already noticed.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2 does not describe Doug's weight loss, thinness, or bags under his eyes.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'Loafer(s)' appears 21 times manuscript-wide, with 11 of those in Ch14 alone (lines include 3508, 3518, 3642, 3648, 3660, 3664). The loafer is a tracked clue (wrong shoes on Cliff's body), but 11 instances in one chapter saturates the image.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch14 uses 'loafer(s)' 11 times. As a clue, the wrong shoes need to register clearly, but 11 repetitions in one chapter diminishes impact. The outline tracks the shoe detail as evidence, but the current density converts a clue into a tic. Target: keep 4-5 strongest instances in Ch14.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce loafer mentions in Ch14 to 4-5. Keep the discovery moment (wrong shoes on the body), Mallick's later analysis, and the contrast with deck shoes. Cut repetitions in transitional paragraphs and reflective passages that re-invoke the shoes without adding information.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 contains no more than 5 uses of 'loafer(s).' Each remaining instance advances the clue or the emotional beat.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'Third stair/step' appears 24 times across 13 chapters. Ch6/1628 re-explains: 'He always forgot about the third step and he always stepped on it, twenty-five years of stepping on that step.' This is the 9th-10th mention, adding explanation the reader already has from Ch1/9, Ch1/449, Ch2/530, Ch2/644, Ch3/793, Ch3/885, Ch4/912.
redundancy_findings.problem: The third stair is a tracked motif (24 mentions, target 8-10), but Ch6/1628 re-explains the detail with a full sentence of backstory ('twenty-five years of stepping on that step') that the reader has already received. This is the sixth chapter in a row to describe the stair.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch6/1628, cut the explanatory sentence about twenty-five years of stepping on it. A simple action verb ('the step creaked under him') is sufficient.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch6 does not explain why the third step is significant or how long Nate has been stepping on it.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'Third stair/step' appears 24 times manuscript-wide. Ch10/2518: 'the same third stair from the top that everybody knew to skip.' This is the ~15th mention, re-explaining a detail the reader has known since Ch1.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch10/2518 re-explains the third stair with 'that everybody knew to skip,' adding no new information after 14 prior mentions. The tracked motif target is 8-10 instances.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch10/2518, remove the explanatory clause 'that everybody knew to skip.' Let the stair creak as action without editorial glossing.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 does not explain that everyone knows about the third stair.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'Something' appears 108 times manuscript-wide (1 per 604 prose words, exceeding the 1-per-800 threshold). Ch2 contains 26 instances in 5,723 words (1 per 220 words), the worst density of any chapter by a wide margin. Examples: 'something awful,' 'something wrong,' 'something that had,' 'something like.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch2 uses 'something' 26 times — more than triple the manuscript average and over 3.5x the acceptable threshold. The hedge creates a pattern of vague deferral that weakens the specificity of Paul's observations in the chapter where precision matters most.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Ch2 to no more than 8 instances (1 per ~700 words). Replace most uses with the specific noun or sensation being deferred. Keep uses where vagueness is the point (e.g., 'something was wrong with Martin' before Paul understands what).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2 contains no more than 8 instances of 'something.' Remaining instances serve deliberate vagueness rather than habitual hedging.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'Something' appears 108 times manuscript-wide (1 per 604 words). Ch14 contains 15 instances in 5,329 words (1 per 355 words), the second-worst chapter.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch14 uses 'something' 15 times, nearly double the manuscript average. In the chapter where the tonal break occurs and precision should intensify, the hedge softens impact.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Ch14 to no more than 6 instances. Replace hedged descriptions with concrete sensory details, especially around the discovery of Cliff's body.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 contains no more than 6 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'Something' appears 108 times manuscript-wide (1 per 604 words). Ch16 contains 6 instances in 2,640 words (1 per 440 words).
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch16 uses 'something' at 1 per 440 words, well above the 1-per-800 threshold. In Joel's deduction chapter, the hedge weakens the logical precision that defines his voice.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Ch16 to no more than 2 instances. Replace with specific nouns or observations.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 contains no more than 2 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'Something' appears 108 times manuscript-wide (1 per 604 words). Ch5 contains 8 instances in 3,828 words (1 per 478 words).
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch5 uses 'something' at 1 per 478 words in Mallick's first POV chapter. Mallick is observationally precise; the hedge undercuts her voice.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Ch5 to no more than 3 instances. Mallick should name what she notices rather than defer to 'something.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5 contains no more than 3 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8/2044 and Ch16/4240 both reproduce Martin's fire document opening: 'I'm writing this down because somebody should, and because I don't trust myself to remember it right if I don't write it now while it's still clear.' The opening sentence is verbatim across chapters because it is the same document. However, the document content then diverges significantly (see consistency finding C01).
redundancy_findings.problem: Martin's fire document is reproduced at length in both Ch8 (lines 2042-2066, ~600 words) and Ch16 (lines 4238-4256, ~500 words). Even accounting for it being the same document, the second full reproduction is a near-verbatim passage that the reader has already absorbed. Ch8 establishes the document; Ch16 should not re-quote it in full.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch16, do not reproduce the full document text. Joel can read silently; the chapter can excerpt only the details NEW to Joel (or that hit differently from his perspective) and summarize the rest in a sentence. The opening line need not be repeated.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 does not quote more than 2-3 sentences of the fire document. Joel's reading is conveyed through reaction and selective excerpt rather than full reproduction.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2042 dates the document 'August 17, 2002' and describes the fire on 'the night of August 11, 2002.' Ch16/4238 dates the same document 'August 14, 2002' with no specific fire-night date. Ch8: kerosene heater knocked over in cabin kitchen, Toomey in back bedroom, fire ruled 'accidental/faulty heater.' Ch16: gasoline can near space heater in 'the shed,' Toomey in the shed, fire ruled 'electrical.' Ch8: Martin drove back alone at 3 AM to reposition heater. Ch16: Martin 'didn't tell the truth in any useful way' but no return trip described.
consistency_findings.problem: Martin's fire document is the same physical document read by Cliff in Ch8 and Joel in Ch16, but the two chapters present it with different dates, different fire causes, different structures (cabin kitchen vs. shed), a different location for the victim (back bedroom vs. shed), and a different fire ruling (accidental/faulty heater vs. electrical). These cannot be perspective differences — the document is one artifact.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile the fire document to a single consistent version. Choose one date, one cause, one structure, one fire ruling, and one version of Martin's role. Propagate the chosen version to both Ch8 and Ch16. The Ch12 Tommy-memory version may intentionally diverge (unreliable memory), but the written document must be internally consistent.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The fire document text in Ch8 and Ch16 matches on: date written, date of fire, structure involved, cause of fire, location of Toomey, and fire ruling.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2008: 'Cliff took the cash first and counted without removing the clip. Twenties and fifties. Roughly two thousand. He put it back.' Ch11/2739: 'A smaller envelope with cash. Paul counted it automatically... Eight hundred and sixty dollars in twenties and fifties.'
consistency_findings.problem: The cash in Martin's safe is 'roughly two thousand' when Cliff counts it (Ch8/2008, Sunday morning) but 'eight hundred and sixty dollars' when Paul counts it (Ch11/2739, Monday). Cliff explicitly puts the cash back. No character is described removing cash between these chapters.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile the cash amount. Either make both counts match (e.g., both ~$860 or both ~$2,000), or add a brief note that someone took cash from the safe between Sunday and Monday if that is the intended plot.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The cash amount in the safe is consistent across Ch8 and Ch11, or an intervening removal is explicitly established.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1/7: 'Nate parked behind Cliff's Audi. The Audi was new, or new-looking.' Ch8/2004: 'Cliff's Lexus. The Lexus was leased. Fourteen hundred a month.'
consistency_findings.problem: Cliff's vehicle is an Audi in Ch1/7 and a Lexus in Ch8/2004. These are different makes. Only one car was driven to the lake house.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Pick one make and correct the other. Given Cliff's financial stress narrative in Ch8 (leased, fourteen hundred a month), the Lexus detail may be more thematically functional. If so, change Ch1/7 'Audi' to 'Lexus.'
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Cliff's car is the same make in Ch1 and Ch8.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2082: 'Cliff photographed each page, front and back, four sheets, eight images.' Ch8/2116: 'what those eight photos were worth.' Ch18/4668: 'Cliff Haywood's phone with fourteen photos of that document.'
consistency_findings.problem: Cliff takes 8 photos (4 sheets x 2 sides) in Ch8, but Ch18 says his phone contained 14 photos of the document. 8 and 14 do not match.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change Ch18/4668 from 'fourteen photos' to 'eight photos' to match the established count in Ch8.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The number of photos on Cliff's phone matches the number taken in Ch8.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch16/4382: 'seven columns of names in different colors from Thursday night' (correct for a 7-man league). Ch18/4878: 'Twelve columns, eighteen rounds, names written in his small steady hand.'
consistency_findings.problem: The draft board has 7 columns in Ch16 (correct — one per league member) but 12 columns in Ch18. A seven-man league would have seven columns.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change Ch18/4878 from 'Twelve columns' to 'Seven columns.'
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The draft board column count in Ch18 matches the number of league members (seven).

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2008: 'Inside were three manila folders, a small law-office envelope, one legal-size manila envelope, and a stack of bills clipped together.' Ch11/2739: 'a manila folder of financial documents... A smaller envelope with cash... A sealed manila envelope, letter-sized... And beneath everything, in a clear plastic sleeve, a document.'
consistency_findings.problem: The safe contents inventory differs between Ch8 and Ch11. Three manila folders become one. The 'legal-size' envelope becomes 'letter-sized.' The 'law-office envelope' disappears. Martin's will (in a clear plastic sleeve) appears in Ch11 but was not mentioned in Ch8, despite Cliff examining the safe contents. The 'stack of bills clipped together' becomes 'a smaller envelope with cash.'
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile the safe contents across Ch8 and Ch11. Either (a) make both inventories match exactly, or (b) if Cliff's inventory is deliberately incomplete (he was focused on the debt), note explicitly in Ch8 that he didn't examine everything. The will's absence from Ch8 is the most critical gap — Cliff should either notice and dismiss it, or the will should not be visible on casual inspection.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The safe contents in Ch8 and Ch11 are reconciled: same number of folders, same envelope size, same cash container, and the will's presence or absence in Ch8 is explained.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2048 (Martin's document): 'His blue Ford pickup was still in the drive Friday night.' Ch12/2986 (Tommy's memory): 'Green Ford pickup. Dull paint. Mud on the wheel wells.'
consistency_findings.problem: Ray Toomey's truck is blue in Martin's written account (Ch8) and green in Tommy's memory (Ch12). The written account was composed days after the event and should be more reliable than a 22-year-old memory. If this is intentional unreliable memory, it is not signaled to the reader.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Either match the truck color across both accounts, or have Tommy's narration acknowledge uncertainty about the detail ('green, maybe blue, the color was already fading in his memory').
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The truck color is consistent, or the discrepancy is acknowledged as memory uncertainty.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2026: describes the RT envelope as 'the legal-size envelope' with 'a metal clasp that had been bent open and closed often enough to loosen it.' Ch11/2739: 'A sealed manila envelope, letter-sized, with the letters RT written on it.' Ch8 says the clasp was loose from repeated opening; Ch11 says the envelope is 'sealed' with 'flap unbroken' (line 2773).
consistency_findings.problem: The RT envelope is 'legal-size' with a loose, oft-opened clasp in Ch8, but 'letter-sized' and 'sealed' with 'flap unbroken' in Ch11. The physical state of the envelope contradicts between chapters — it cannot be both repeatedly opened and sealed/unbroken.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Decide the canonical state: was the envelope previously opened (Ch8's loose clasp) or sealed (Ch11's unbroken flap)? If Cliff opened it in Ch8, Paul should find it re-closed but not sealed. Reconcile size to one designation.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The RT envelope has a consistent size and seal state across Ch8 and Ch11.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2008-2014: Cliff inventories the safe and finds three folders (HOUSE, INVESTMENTS, PERSONAL), the RT envelope, a law-office envelope, and cash. He opens and reads the RT envelope. Ch11/2739: Paul finds 'a manila folder of financial documents... A sealed manila envelope, letter-sized... a document [the will] in a clear plastic sleeve.'
consistency_findings.problem: Cliff examined the safe contents in detail in Ch8 but did not see or mention Martin's will. Paul finds the will in Ch11 in a 'clear plastic sleeve' beneath everything. If the will was in the safe when Cliff opened it Sunday morning, Cliff either saw it and ignored it (implausible given his financial motive) or it was not there yet (impossible — no one else put it in). The most likely explanation is that the will was omitted from Ch8 in error.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Add the will to Cliff's inventory in Ch8. Cliff can notice it, register that it is Martin's will, and choose not to read it carefully because he is focused on the debt documentation and the RT envelope. This preserves the plot while closing the gap.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The will appears in Cliff's safe inventory in Ch8, even if he does not read it in detail.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2058-2060 (Martin's document): 'Martin had driven back at three in the morning. Alone. Work gloves from the trunk. He repositioned the heater to make it look like it failed where it stood. He adjusted what he could of the spill pattern. The fire investigation ruled it accidental.' Ch16/4248 (Martin's document): 'The fire department ruled it electrical. Tommy told them what he told me. I didn't contradict him.'
consistency_findings.problem: In the Ch8 version of Martin's document, Martin returns alone to the scene at 3 AM and physically tampers with evidence (repositions the heater, adjusts the spill). In the Ch16 version, Martin merely stays silent ('I didn't contradict him'). This changes Martin's culpability from active evidence tampering to passive omission — a significant difference for the moral weight of the story.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: This is part of the same fire-document reconciliation as C01. Choose one version of Martin's role (active tampering or passive silence) and apply it consistently across Ch8 and Ch16.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Martin's described role in the fire aftermath is the same in both Ch8 and Ch16 versions of the document.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8/2048: Tommy had been 'drinking since they'd left Minneapolis.' Ch16/4242: 'Tommy was drinking heavy on Saturday, worse than usual, and usual was already a lot. Around 9 PM he went outside to smoke.' Ch12/2972: 'Friday night. Bonfire. Too much lighter fluid.'
consistency_findings.problem: The fire document in Ch8 says they arrived and the fire happened the same night (Tommy drinking 'since they'd left Minneapolis'). The Ch16 version says they'd been there 'since Friday' and the fire happened Saturday night. Ch12 Tommy's memory says 'Friday night.' The day of the fire is inconsistent across all three versions.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile as part of the fire-document reconciliation (C01). The document should specify one consistent day. Tommy's memory in Ch12 may diverge intentionally but should not contradict the document on basic timeline.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The day the fire occurs is consistent between the two renderings of Martin's document. Tommy's memory in Ch12 either matches or is flagged as uncertain.
